title: Needle-felted seal
subtitle: A compact grey Atlantic seal in merino, smooth-bodied with wire-armature flippers
excerpt: Build a plump Atlantic seal in mid-grey and pale-cream merino, using a wire armature in each flipper so they pose naturally. The finished seal is about 12 cm long and sits securely on its belly.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from traditional needle-felting practice. Compact marine-mammal forms are a standard British needle-felting beginner project; the wire-armature flipper technique follows standard guild workshop method.

glossaryTerms.definition: A dry felting method using barbed needles to tangle wool fibres together without heat or water. Each stab locks fibres into a progressively firmer mass.
Wool fibre drawn into a continuous ribbon. The standard input material for needle felting.
A wire frame inside a needle-felted piece that allows limbs and appendages to be posed. Thin florists wire or craft wire serves for small pieces.

content.text: is a smooth oval body with four flat flippers and a broad muzzle. The
in the front flippers is what makes the pose work: without it, flippers droop. Use grey
for the body and pale cream or white for the muzzle and belly patch. Beginners can skip the armature and felt flat flippers instead.

attrs.title: Needle safety
attrs.body: Stab straight in and out at 90 degrees. Keep a finger guard on the supporting hand at all times.

items.name: Pale cream or white merino roving
Thin florists or craft wire (0.4 mm)
Strong thread and needle for bead eyes

content.text: Take a generous handful of grey roving and roll it firmly into a sausage shape about 15 cm long. Needle felt on all sides, turning constantly, until the body is firm and about 12 cm long. The belly is flatter than the back; press the piece face-down on the mat and needle the belly flat while the back stays rounded.
Step 2: Add the muzzle and belly

Pull a small amount of cream roving and needle it into a rounded blob at one end of the body for the muzzle, then a broader patch along the underside for the belly. Work the edges inward so the colour blends at the boundary rather than stopping abruptly.

Cut two 12 cm lengths of wire. Wrap each in a small amount of grey roving, securing the roving by needling along the length. Fold the padded wire into a flipper shape: a flat oval with a slight point at the tip. Needle felt more roving over the wire until the flipper is smooth and firm. Attach each flipper to the body at the shoulder, needling through both flipper base and body to join them securely.

Needle two small flat paddle shapes from grey roving for the hind flippers. These are simpler than the front flippers and need no wire. Attach them to the back end of the body, one on each side, angling them backward. The hind flippers are smaller and do not pose; they stabilise the seal on its belly.

Needle two small indentations in the muzzle for nostrils. Stitch the black seed bead eyes in position just above and behind the muzzle using strong thread, passing through the body and pulling tight so the beads sit close to the surface without sinking. Trim any loose fibres with small scissors.

items.problem: The body is lumpy and uneven
items.cause: The roving was not turned regularly during needling, so one side compacted more than the other
items.fix: Rotate the piece onto a new face every few minutes and needle evenly on all sides from the start.

items.problem: The flipper wire pokes through the surface
items.cause: Insufficient roving wrapped over the wire before needling, or the needling cut through the roving too aggressively
items.fix: Add more roving over any thin areas and needle gently until the surface is smooth and the wire is no longer visible.
